text: In nature, enzymatic heme and non-heme high-valent iron-oxo species readily activate strong C–H bonds; however, recreating this potent reactivity within synthetic systems remains a significant challenge. This difficulty stems not only from the inherent instability of the terminal iron-oxo moiety but also from the steric and electronic constraints that frequently hamper the performance of synthetic mononuclear iron(IV)-oxo complexes. Crucially, while biological non-heme iron catalysts utilize high-spin (S = 2) ground states to achieve their remarkable activity, this electronic configuration is notoriously difficult to stabilize in isolated molecular species. We demonstrate that these limitations can be overcome by using metal-organic frameworks to provide site-isolated iron centers within a constrained, weak-field ligand environment. Specifically, the framework Fe2(dobdc) and its magnesium-diluted variant, Fe0.1Mg1.9(dobdc), facilitate the catalytic oxidation of ethane to ethanol and acetaldehyde using nitrous oxide as a terminal oxidant. Electronic structure calculations suggest that this reactivity is driven by a high-spin S = 2 iron(IV)-oxo intermediate, mimicking the strategy employed by nature’s most effective oxidation catalysts.
